content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886


February 8, 2002


Gene H. Kezirian, Treasurer
Phil Larson for Supervisor
Post Office Box 92
Kerman, CA 93630

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-02-004

Dear Mr. Kezirian:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

When a candidate is running in two elections on a single day for two seats on the
county board of supervisors, and the campaign for both seats is funded by just one of the
two committees, must each committee reflect on its Form 460 a pro rata share of the
receipts and expenditures for the campaign?

CONCLUSION

You must accurately record all receipts and expenditures of both committees
formed for the elections held on March 5, 2002. You are not required to apportion
between these committees the actual financial activities of one committee, if the
candidate chooses to run a single campaign for supervisor through just one of the two
committees. However, you should refer, on the special election committee's Form 460,
to the other committee's campaign report, to avoid a misleading suggestion on the one
form that the candidate raised and spent nothing on the March 5, 2002 supervisorial race.


¹ Government Code sections 81000 – 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. A-02-004
Page No. 2

FACTS

You are treasurer for Phil Larson for Supervisor. Mr. Larson is running for a seat
on the Fresno County Board of Supervisors in a regularly scheduled election to be held
on March 5, 2002. Due to the recent death of an incumbent supervisor, a special election
will be held to fill the remaining term of that seat. This special election will also be held
on March 5, 2002. Mr. Larson is now running for this recently vacated seat in the special
election, as well as for the full-term seat in the regularly scheduled election taking place
on the same day.

Mr. Larson has formed a second committee for the special election, and will file a
Form 460 for that committee. But he has not changed his campaign plans now that he
has become a candidate for two offices. That is to say, he does not intend to mount a
separate advertising campaign for the special election. He believes that his campaign, as
originally designed for the full-term seat, will deliver an equally effective message to the
same pool of voters he needs to reach (at the same time) for election to the limited-term
seat. Therefore you intend to receive contributions into the special election committee
only to the extent necessary to pay the costs of establishing a candidacy for the limited-
term seat, specifically the filing fee and ballot pamphlet costs. You do not expect to
receive other contributions into that committee, or to make any expenditures from that
committee's account, except the two mentioned above.

A committee must disclose on the Form 460 all of its receipts and disbursements.
In this case, you anticipate that only one committee will actually receive contributions
and make expenditures advocating the candidate's election to the board of supervisors.
As a practical matter, the candidate expects to run in both elections on the strength of a
single election campaign funded by one committee. The problem is simply how to reflect
this reality on the two Form 460's that you will have filed.

ANALYSIS

The Act requires that before any contributions may be solicited or received for
election to any office, the candidate must file a candidate intention statement (Form 501),
identifying the office sought and the date of the election. (§ 85200.) Further, every
committee which raises $1,000 or more must file a statement of organization (Form 410,
§ 84101), and a campaign statement (Form 460, §§ 84200 et seq.).) Candidates must
establish a campaign bank account for each office sought, and identify that bank account
on their Form 410. (§ 85201.) We assume that the special election committee will be
required to file these statements.

The Form 460 requires a detailed accounting of a committee's campaign
contributions and expenditures. Occasionally, candidates form two committees for
elections held on the same day. In these cases it may be difficult to attribute expenditures
to one election or the other. In the Olson Advice Letter, No. A-96-088, we addressed a
similar situation. In that case, a candidate was running in special and primary elections
on the same day for different terms of the same office. We observed that, because the

File No. A-02-004
Page No. 3

campaign period for the two elections would be identical, in many instances it would be
difficult, if not impossible, to distinguish expenditures made for the primary from those
made for the special election. We assumed in that case that certain expenses would be
attributable to both campaigns, but left it to the candidate to work out the allocation
appropriate under all the facts and circumstances "using any reasonable method."

In your case, you report that the candidate intends to conduct his campaign in
precisely the same manner anticipated before the second seat became vacant. We
understand that, besides running all contributions and expenditures through a single
account, Mr. Larson will not produce campaign materials specifically for the special
election, and will not otherwise make an expenditure that could reasonably be considered
as specific to the special election. In effect, all expenditures of the committee would be
directed to advocating "Larson for Supervisor," without particular reference to either
election.

If the campaign is indeed conducted in this fashion, we next ask whether it is a
"reasonable method" to attribute all campaign expenditures (except special election fees)
to the committee originally established for the supervisorial race. We interpret what is
"reasonable" in this context by reference to the fundamental purposes of the Act, one of
which is full and truthful disclosure of campaign receipts and expenditures. (§ 81002(a).)
We believe that, under the circumstances you describe, it is consistent with the purposes
of the Act to list on one committee's campaign report all expenditures not specifically
assignable to one "election" or the other.

It is possible that such an allocation method might be misleading insofar as one
committee's Form 460 would suggest that the candidate had raised and spent almost
nothing on the March 5, 2002 supervisorial election. This difficulty could be overcome if
the special election committee's Form 460 refers the reader to the other committee's
report for a full account of receipts and expenditures in the simultaneous campaign.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
